does: This paper introduces a debate framework for AI safety in which two agents argue opposing positions on a topic while a human judge evaluates their arguments to determine the winner. The core contribution is a training method where agents learn to construct persuasive arguments, anticipate counterarguments, and identify flaws in their opponent's reasoning. The approach leverages the competitive dynamic between debaters to surface relevant information and evidence that might otherwise remain hidden, with the goal of producing more truthful and justified outputs than either agent would generate independently. The method is evaluated on whether human judges can reliably identify correct positions even on complex topics where they lack complete expertise.
connects: This work builds on prior research in AI alignment and interpretability that seeks to make AI systems more transparent and trustworthy. It extends ideas from debate and argumentation theory into the machine learning domain, drawing on classical work in human persuasion and rhetorical reasoning. The paper responds to fundamental challenges in AI safety around scalable oversight and human-AI collaboration, connecting to concurrent research on iterated amplification and recursive reward modeling that also aim to evaluate complex AI outputs. The debate framing can be seen as a novel approach to the problem of training agents whose objectives align with human values and understanding, addressing limitations of direct human evaluation on tasks that exceed human capability.
enables: This framework opens pathways for training AI systems that are more interpretable and auditable through their explicit reasoning processes. The approach suggests methods for handling cases where human judges cannot directly verify correctness but can evaluate arguments and evidence quality, potentially enabling oversight of AI systems working on domains beyond human expertise. Future work could investigate scaling debate to longer and more complex arguments, combining debate with other techniques like interpretability tools or auxiliary explanations, and studying how debate performance correlates with actual accuracy and reliability. The method also points toward recursive applications where AI systems debate more sophisticated questions, and toward developing AI systems that can explain and justify their reasoning in ways humans can scrutinize.
